Were You Asked This?
Design a file system for write-once media

Design a file system that can efficiently store and manage files on write-once media, where data can only be written once and cannot be modified after creation.

Asked at:

Google

A write-once file system is a storage layer for media where bytes can only be appended and never overwritten (e.g., WORM drives, optical media, tape, or "object lock" modes in cloud storage). Users write files, list directories, and read data back, but any change is modeled as a new version that points to new immutable blocks plus updated metadata.

Interviewers ask this to see whether you can adapt familiar file system ideas (inodes, directories, journals) to a constrained environment. It tests your ability to design append-only data structures, atomic publish protocols, indexing for fast reads, integrity checks, and recovery after crashes—without relying on in-place mutation. It also probes your judgment around deletion semantics, retention, and techniques like content-addressable storage and crypto-shredding.

Community Solutions
Design a file system for write-once media
by Gajendra Waghmare
4
1
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to create and store files of arbitrary size with atomic publish semantics (a file is either fully visible or not visible).

Users should be able to efficiently read files (including partial and random reads) and list directories at scale.

Users should be able to manage logical updates and deletions via versioning and tombstones, while honoring write-once constraints.

Users should be able to verify file integrity and optionally deduplicate identical content via content hashes.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you provide atomic file creation and directory updates on write-once media?

How will you handle updates and deletions when bytes cannot be overwritten (retention, GDPR, and space management)?

How do you keep reads fast at scale when metadata and data are append-only?

What is your crash-recovery story and how do you guarantee integrity across large files?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Handling Large Blobs

Files can be very large, so you need chunking, manifests, checksums, and possibly erasure coding. The Large Blobs pattern guides how to split, stream, and verify data without in-place updates.

Read More
Scaling Reads

All metadata is append-only, so efficient reads rely on auxiliary indexes, caching, and precomputed manifests to avoid scanning cold segments. The Scaling Reads pattern helps structure these read paths.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

Publishing a file requires multiple durable steps: write chunks, write manifest, update directory, and advance a checkpoint. Multi-Step Processes (sagas, idempotency, checkpoints) ensure atomicity and recovery.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka’s append-only segmented log is a strong mental model for the on-media journal of metadata and manifests. Concepts like offsets, segment indexes, and idempotent producers map well to write-once commit flows.

Read More
ZooKeeper

ZooKeeper can coordinate leadership, allocation ranges, and epoch advancement for checkpoint pointers, ensuring only one writer publishes a new snapshot and enabling safe recovery after failures.

Read More
PostgreSQL

A Postgres-backed mutable index can serve fast path lookups (path → manifest/chunk pointers) and directory listings, while being rebuildable from the append-only journal to preserve source-of-truth immutability.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Dropbox

Both systems store large files as chunks with manifests and need versioning, deduplication, and integrity verification. The difference is write-once media removes in-place metadata mutation, pushing you to pure copy-on-write.

Read
Practice
YouTube

Video upload pipelines also chunk and persist large immutable blobs, then publish metadata indexes for fast reads and partial streaming. Atomic publish and background verification are shared concerns.

Read
Practice
Ad Click Aggregator

Click pipelines rely on append-only logs and immutable records, then build queryable indexes for fast reads. This mirrors append-only metadata journals with rebuildable secondary indexes in a write-once file system.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Assuming in-place updates to files or directories

Designs that overwrite metadata blocks or modify files in place violate the core write-once constraint and lead to impossible operations, undefined behavior, or data loss after crashes.

No clear atomic publish or recovery mechanism

If you cannot explain how a file becomes visible atomically and how the system recovers from partial writes, the design risks corrupt directories, dangling pointers, and inconsistent reads after failures.

Ignoring deletion/retention and privacy requirements

Saying you will simply delete bytes later is incompatible with WORM media. You need tombstones, versioning, and crypto-shredding; otherwise, you cannot satisfy legal deletion or control storage growth.
